title: Zoology & Animal Behavior
subtitle: Animal diversity, ecology & the evolution of behaviour
methodology: METHODOLOGY · Every entry anchors to a landmark study, field observation or theoretical text. The hint explains the idea in plain language; the note names the primary publication and flags replication status, effect-size revisions and known controversies. Studies and effects carry a replicability badge — 🟢 robust · 🟡 nuanced or reduced · 🔴 failed or severely contested. Map pins mark the institution where the work was carried out.

categories.label: Theories & Models
categories.subtitle: The frameworks that organise how animal behaviour evolved and is maintained

entries.label: Natural Selection & Adaptation
entries.hint: Heritable variation in traits that affect survival and reproduction causes those advantageous traits to increase in frequency over generations — the mechanism of evolution. For zoology, this means every anatomical, physiological and behavioural feature of an animal is the product of selection acting on ancestors, and can be interrogated as an adaptation to a specific environmental challenge.
entries.note: Darwin, C. R. (1859). On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection. John Murray, London. The modern synthesis integrated Mendelian genetics with natural selection (Huxley 1942; Dobzhansky 1937). Adaptionism critique: Gould, S. J. & Lewontin, R. C. (1979). 'The Spandrels of San Marco.' Proceedings of the Royal Society B, 205, 581–598 — not all traits are adaptive; some are developmental by-products.

entries.label: Inclusive Fitness & Kin Selection
entries.hint: An individual maximises not just its own reproduction but the propagation of the genes it shares with relatives — its inclusive fitness. Hamilton's rule states that an altruistic act spreads when rB > C: the genetic relatedness (r) times the benefit to the recipient (B) exceeds the cost to the actor (C). This explains why worker bees sacrifice reproduction, why ground squirrels alarm-call at personal risk, and why sibling rivalry has predictable limits.
entries.note: Hamilton, W. D. (1964). 'The genetical evolution of social behaviour I & II.' Journal of Theoretical Biology, 7, 1–52. Coined by Maynard Smith (1964) as 'kin selection.' Empirically tested across insects, birds and mammals — see Sherman (1977) on ground squirrels. Controversy: Nowak, Tarnita & Wilson (2010) Nature challenged inclusive fitness as the necessary framework for eusociality; this triggered intense rebuttal from ~130 co-signatories.

entries.label: Evolutionarily Stable Strategy (ESS)
entries.hint: A strategy is evolutionarily stable if, when adopted by most of the population, no mutant strategy can invade it. Maynard Smith applied game theory to animal conflict to show why animals often display rather than fight to the death — if costly fights are common, a 'dove' strategy can invade a population of 'hawks', converging on a mixed equilibrium. ESS replaced the assumption that animals evolve 'for the good of the species'.
entries.note: Maynard Smith, J. & Price, G. R. (1973). 'The logic of animal conflict.' Nature, 246, 15–18. Extended in Maynard Smith, J. (1982). Evolution and the Theory of Games. Cambridge University Press. The framework was formalised from Price's covariance equation and has been applied to sex ratios, foraging, parasite virulence and communication. Empirically robust across taxa: meta-analysis confirms mixed ESS predictions in territorial contests.

entries.label: Optimal Foraging Theory
entries.hint: Animals are expected to forage in ways that maximise net energy gain per unit time. The prey model predicts which food types to include in the diet given encounter rates and handling costs; the patch model predicts when to leave a depleting patch (Charnov's marginal value theorem: leave when the instantaneous reward rate drops to the habitat average). These models generate precise, testable predictions about diet breadth and patch residence times.
entries.note: MacArthur, R. H. & Pianka, E. R. (1966). 'On optimal use of a patchy environment.' American Naturalist, 100, 603–609. Patch model: Charnov, E. L. (1976). 'Optimal foraging: the marginal value theorem.' Theoretical Population Biology, 9, 129–136. Meta-analysis: Sih, A. & Christensen, B. (2001) Oikos found 53% of studies showed quantitative agreement — predictions are directionally correct but animals routinely deviate, especially under predation risk.

entries.label: Sexual Selection Theory
entries.hint: Traits that reduce survival can spread if they improve mating success. Darwin distinguished intrasexual selection (male-male competition — antlers, fighting, territory) from intersexual selection (female choice — peacock tails, birdsong, elaborate displays). The sexes are predicted to diverge whenever the variance in reproductive success differs between them, creating asymmetric selection pressure on males versus females.
entries.note: Darwin, C. R. (1871). The Descent of Man, and Selection in Relation to Sex. John Murray, London. Runaway selection model: Fisher, R. A. (1930) — sexy-son hypothesis. Good genes models: Zahavi (1975) handicap principle. Modern synthesis: Andersson, M. (1994). Sexual Selection. Princeton University Press. Cross-species meta-analysis: Andersson & Simmons (2006) Trends in Ecology & Evolution confirms strong intersexual selection in polygynous species.

entries.label: Handicap Principle
entries.loc: Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Jerusalem, Israel
entries.hint: Reliable signalling requires that signals be costly to produce, because only high-quality individuals can afford the cost without net fitness loss. A peacock's tail, a stag's roar and a gazelle's stotting display are honest precisely because they impose real energetic or survival costs — cheap signals would be faked by low-quality individuals and would lose their informational value. The handicap principle resolved the paradox of honest communication between competing agents.
entries.note: Zahavi, A. (1975). 'Mate selection — a selection for a handicap.' Journal of Theoretical Biology, 53, 205–214. Formal proof: Grafen, A. (1990). 'Biological signals as handicaps.' Journal of Theoretical Biology, 144, 517–546. Controversy: costly signalling vs index signalling — Maynard Smith & Harper (2003) Animal Signals argue many reliable signals are not costly handicaps but morphological indices (e.g. body size). Empirically supported for bird plumage and mammal vocalisations.

entries.label: Parental Investment Theory
entries.hint: Parental investment is any resource expenditure that benefits offspring at a cost to the parent's ability to invest in future offspring. The sex that invests more per offspring will be the choosier sex; the sex that invests less competes more intensely for mates. In most mammals the female is the high-investing sex (gestation, lactation), predicting female choosiness and male-male competition — but in sex-role-reversed species like seahorses, the predictions flip.
entries.note: Trivers, R. L. (1972). 'Parental investment and sexual selection.' In Campbell, B. (Ed.), Sexual Selection and the Descent of Man 1871–1971. Aldine, Chicago, 136–179. Extended Bateman's (1948) principle. Predictions tested in polyandrous birds (e.g. jacanas: Jenni & Collier 1972) and pipefish (Berglund et al. 1989). Critique: Tang-Martinez & Ryder (2005) argue the theory is gender-biased and that 'investment' is poorly operationalised.

entries.label: Reciprocal Altruism
entries.hint: Costly help to unrelated individuals can be maintained by natural selection if the recipient reliably helps in return, and if cheaters are detected and excluded. The theory predicts help between non-relatives in long-lived, cognitively sophisticated species where individuals repeatedly interact — vampire bats sharing blood meals, cleaner fish, reciprocal grooming in primates. Crucially, it requires memory, individual recognition and enforcement against defection.
entries.note: Trivers, R. L. (1971). 'The evolution of reciprocal altruism.' Quarterly Review of Biology, 46, 35–57. Game-theoretic formalisation: Axelrod, R. & Hamilton, W. D. (1981). 'The evolution of cooperation.' Science, 211, 1390–1396. Empirical cases: vampire bat food sharing (Wilkinson 1984); cleaner fish (Bshary & Grutter 2006). Critique: Clutton-Brock (2009) Animal Behaviour argues most 'reciprocal altruism' in animals is better explained by by-product mutualism.

entries.label: Tinbergen's Four Questions
entries.hint: Any complete explanation of a behaviour must address four distinct but complementary questions: (1) mechanism — what stimuli and neural processes cause it now?; (2) ontogeny — how did it develop across the individual's lifetime?; (3) function — how does it contribute to survival and reproduction?; (4) phylogeny — how did it evolve across lineages? Confusing levels of explanation account for most unproductive debates in animal behaviour and beyond.
entries.note: Tinbergen, N. (1963). 'On aims and methods of ethology.' Zeitschrift für Tierpsychologie, 20, 410–433. Anticipated by Huxley (1914) and formalised from Lorenz-Tinbergen tradition. The framework was extended to human psychology by Scott et al. (2017) Frontiers in Psychology. Critiqued as falsely implying independence among levels — mechanistic and evolutionary questions in fact constrain each other.

entries.label: Red Queen Hypothesis
entries.hint: Species must continuously evolve just to maintain their fitness relative to co-evolving adversaries — parasites, pathogens, competitors and prey. Van Valen noted that extinction risk appears roughly constant over geological time regardless of how long a lineage has existed, suggesting evolution never achieves a permanent advantage. The hypothesis explains the persistence of sex (recombination generates parasite-resistant genotypes faster than asexual clones) and the arms-race dynamics between hosts and parasites.
entries.note: Van Valen, L. (1973). 'A new evolutionary law.' Evolutionary Theory, 1, 1–30. Extended to host-parasite arms races: Hamilton, W. D., Axelrod, R. & Tanese, R. (1990) PNAS. Empirical support for the sex hypothesis: Lively, C. M. (1987) Nature — New Zealand snail populations more sexual where parasites are common. Contested: extinction rates do not show the constant pattern Van Valen claimed in all datasets.

entries.label: Life History Theory
entries.loc: University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan
entries.hint: Organisms face a fundamental trade-off: every unit of energy or time invested in one life-history trait (growth, maintenance, current reproduction, offspring quality, parental care) is unavailable for others. Life history theory predicts how natural selection resolves these trade-offs given ecological constraints — fast-living species (high predation, short lifespan) are predicted to mature early, produce many cheap offspring and invest little per offspring; slow-living species (low predation, long lifespan) are predicted to mature late, reproduce rarely and invest heavily. r/K selection, the Stearns trade-off framework, and pace-of-life syndromes are all applications of this core logic.
entries.note: Stearns, S. C. (1977). 'The evolution of life history traits: a critique of the theory and a review of the data.' Annual Review of Ecology and Systematics, 8, 145–171. Full treatment: Stearns, S. C. (1992). The Evolution of Life Histories. Oxford University Press; Roff, D. A. (1992). The Evolution of Life Histories. Chapman & Hall. r/K selection: MacArthur & Wilson (1967) — though largely replaced by Stearns' trade-off framework. Pace-of-life syndromes integrating behaviour and physiology: Réale et al. (2010) Philosophical Transactions B. Empirical tests: guppy life history in high vs low predation streams (Reznick et al. 1990 Science) provide clean experimental evidence.

categories.label: Landmark Studies
categories.subtitle: The defining experiments — and exactly what they found

entries.label: Lorenz on Imprinting in Greylag Geese
entries.hint: Newly hatched greylag goslings will follow and socially bond to the first large moving object they encounter during a sensitive developmental window — normally their mother, but in Lorenz's experiments, Lorenz himself. The bond formed rapidly and was largely irreversible after the critical period closed, demonstrating that some learning is constrained by developmental timing rather than occurring freely across the lifespan.
entries.note: Lorenz, K. (1935). 'Der Kumpan in der Umwelt des Vogels.' Journal für Ornithologie, 83, 137–213, 289–413. The sensitive period concept was confirmed across many species. Revised: imprinting is more reversible than Lorenz claimed, and 'critical period' has been replaced by 'sensitive period' in most accounts. Sluckin, W. (1965) Imprinting and Early Learning provides a comprehensive review. The sexual imprinting effect (mate preference formed on caretaker) is also robust.

entries.label: von Frisch Decodes the Honeybee Waggle Dance
entries.hint: Returning forager bees perform a figure-eight waggle dance on the vertical honeycomb that encodes the direction (relative to the sun) and distance of a food source. The angle of the waggle run relative to vertical maps the azimuth of the target relative to the sun; the duration of waggling encodes distance. This was the first demonstration of symbolic, displaced reference — communication about something absent — in a non-human animal.
entries.note: von Frisch, K. (1946). 'Die Tänze der Bienen.' Österreichische Zoologische Zeitschrift, 1, 1–48. Full account: von Frisch, K. (1967). The Dance Language and Orientation of Bees. Belknap Press. Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1973 (shared with Lorenz and Tinbergen). Challenged by Wenner (1971) who argued odour alone guides recruits; rebutted by Gould (1975) in a deception experiment that blocked odour cues — dances remained effective. Dialect variation among subspecies confirmed (Dyer & Seeley 1991).

entries.label: Tinbergen's Stickleback Experiments
entries.hint: Male three-spined sticklebacks develop a red belly during breeding season and will attack any red-bellied intruder — but ignore a perfectly realistic model lacking red coloration. Tinbergen systematically varied the features of dummy fish to identify the key sign stimulus triggering each Fixed Action Pattern: territorial aggression, courtship display, and nest-building. The experiments established the ethological method of stimulus-response analysis and the concept of the releaser.
entries.note: Tinbergen, N. (1951). The Study of Instinct. Clarendon Press, Oxford. Replication concerns: Rowland (1994) found the red-belly effect is weaker and more context-dependent than Tinbergen described. More serious: Wootton (1984) showed the original wax models differed in multiple features simultaneously, making clean stimulus-isolation impossible. The qualitative finding (colour matters for aggression) is solid; the claim of a single releasing stimulus is oversimplified.

entries.label: Vervet Monkey Semantic Alarm Calls
entries.hint: Vervet monkeys produce acoustically distinct alarm calls for different predator classes — eagles elicit a short tonal call causing vervets to look upward and run into bushes; leopards elicit a loud bark causing them to climb trees; snakes elicit a high-pitched chutter causing them to stand bipedally and scan the ground. Playback of the calls alone produced the predator-appropriate response, demonstrating that the calls carry semantic content — they refer to external categories, not just the caller's arousal state.
entries.note: Seyfarth, R. M., Cheney, D. L. & Marler, P. (1980). 'Monkey responses to three different alarm calls: evidence of predator classification and semantic communication.' Science, 210, 801–803. Extended in Cheney, D. L. & Seyfarth, R. M. (1990). How Monkeys See the World. University of Chicago Press. Replicated for Diana monkeys (Zuberbühler 2000) and prairie dogs (Slobodchikoff 2009, though the prairie dog claims remain contested). The finding of functionally referential calls is cross-taxa robust.

entries.label: Sherman: Alarm Calls in Belding's Ground Squirrels
entries.hint: Female Belding's ground squirrels alarm-call at approaching predators far more often than males, and their call rates correlate with the number of relatives nearby — daughters, sisters and mothers increase call frequency; non-relative females and males do not. Because callers suffer elevated predation risk, the pattern is consistent with kin selection: females are helping relatives at personal cost. Males disperse at maturity and are unrelated to neighbours, explaining their silence.
entries.note: Sherman, P. W. (1977). 'Nepotism and the evolution of alarm calls.' Science, 197, 1246–1253. Extended: Sherman, P. W. (1985). 'Alarm calls of Belding's ground squirrels to aerial predators.' Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology, 17, 313–323. Kin recognition mechanism confirmed (Holmes & Sherman 1982). Considered the strongest field test of Hamilton's rule in vertebrates. Critiqued by Zahavi (1980) who argued the calls could be selfish (scatter the group to escape), but the female-only pattern resists this alternative explanation.

entries.label: Axelrod & Hamilton: The Evolution of Cooperation
entries.loc: University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan
entries.hint: A computer round-robin tournament of iterated Prisoner's Dilemma strategies found that Tit-for-Tat — cooperate on the first move, then copy the opponent's last move — outcompeted all others including complex strategists. The simplest cooperative strategy wins because it is nice (never defects first), retaliatory (punishes defection), forgiving (resumes cooperation once the other does), and clear (opponents learn its rules quickly). This showed cooperation can be individually advantageous without kin selection.
entries.note: Axelrod, R. & Hamilton, W. D. (1981). 'The evolution of cooperation.' Science, 211, 1390–1396. Book: Axelrod, R. (1984). The Evolution of Cooperation. Basic Books. Second tournament confirmed Tit-for-Tat's dominance. However, subsequent work (Nowak & May 1992 Nature; Press & Dyson 2012 PNAS) showed that in spatial or population-size-variable scenarios, Generous Tit-for-Tat or zero-determinant strategies outperform basic TFT. The core insight — reciprocity stabilises cooperation — remains robust.

entries.label: Endler's Guppy Sexual Selection Experiment
entries.hint: Male guppies are far more colourful in low-predation streams than in high-predation streams, because conspicuous colour attracts females but also predators. Endler transplanted drab high-predation guppies into a predator-free pool: within 15 to 20 generations, males had evolved showier colouration. Removing predation allowed female choice for more colourful males to dominate, demonstrating the antagonistic interaction between natural and sexual selection in real time.
entries.note: Endler, J. A. (1980). 'Natural selection on color patterns in Poecilia reticulata.' Evolution, 34, 76–91. Followed up by Reznick et al. (1990) Science — guppies also evolve faster maturation and smaller clutches under high predation within 30–60 generations. One of the most cited demonstrations of evolution in action. Lab and field combined. Reznick, D. A. & Endler, J. A. (1982) Evolution confirmed that colour variance is primarily genetic rather than phenotypically plastic.

entries.label: Davies & Brooke: Cuckoo-Host Arms Race
entries.hint: Common cuckoos lay eggs that closely mimic those of their specific host species, while hosts have evolved egg rejection behaviour. Davies and Brooke compared recognition behaviour across host species with different evolutionary histories of cuckoo parasitism: species with long cuckoo exposure reject foreign eggs at high rates; species recently exposed accept them. This is direct evidence of a coevolutionary arms race — each escalation by the parasite selects for stronger host defences.
entries.note: Davies, N. B. & Brooke, M. de L. (1989). 'An experimental study of co-evolution between the cuckoo, Cuculus canorus, and its hosts.' Journal of Animal Ecology, 58, 207–224, 225–236. Extended in Davies, N. B. (2000). Cuckoos, Cowbirds and Other Cheats. T & AD Poyser. Genetic basis of egg mimicry confirmed: females of each cuckoo race inherit the egg pattern through the W chromosome (Fossøy et al. 2016 Nature Genetics). Brood parasite arms races are now a model system for studying coevolution.

entries.label: Hunt: Tool Manufacture in New Caledonian Crows
entries.hint: New Caledonian crows fashion hooked tools from Pandanus leaf strips by tearing a tapered, barbed length of leaf with the bill and then bending it into a hook — a sequence requiring planning and manipulation. They also create tools from novel materials in lab settings, modify tools in response to task demands, and in some populations show cultural variation in tool form. Tool manufacture (as opposed to tool use) was previously considered uniquely human.
entries.note: Hunt, G. R. (1996). 'Manufacture and use of hook-tools by New Caledonian crows.' Nature, 379, 249–251. Lab confirmation: Weir, A. A. S., Chappell, J. & Kacelnik, A. (2002) Science — spontaneous hook-bending from wire. Meta-tool use (using a tool to get another tool): Taylor et al. (2007) Current Biology. Cultural variation in tool design across populations: Hunt & Gray (2003) Animal Cognition. Ongoing debate about whether causal understanding or reinforcement learning accounts for the behaviour (Taylor et al. 2010).

entries.label: Emlen's Star-Compass in Migratory Birds
entries.hint: Indigo buntings raised in a planetarium with a rotating artificial sky centred on Betelgeuse rather than Polaris oriented their autumn migration relative to that artificial pole — not the real one. Those raised under a stationary sky (no rotation) showed no consistent orientation. Young birds learn which stars rotate least as a proxy for celestial north during the nestling period, then use this as a compass during migration. The star compass is learned, not innate.
entries.note: Emlen, S. T. (1967). 'Migratory orientation in the indigo bunting, Passerina cyanea.' Auk, 84, 309–342, 463–489. Confirmed: Emlen, S. T. (1970) Science demonstrated the learned nature of the reference point. Magnetic compass also demonstrated (Wiltschko & Wiltschko 1972). Multi-cue navigation hierarchy: birds calibrate magnetic compass against star compass during development (Able & Able 1990 Nature). European robins use a light-dependent magnetic compass in the eye (Ritz et al. 2000 Nature).

entries.label: Grant: Finch Beak Evolution After Drought
entries.loc: Isla Daphne Major, Galápagos Islands, Ecuador
entries.hint: A severe 1977 drought on Daphne Major eliminated small, soft seeds and left only large, hard seeds on the island. Medium ground finches with deeper, stronger beaks survived at significantly higher rates; mean beak depth in the post-drought cohort was 0.5 mm greater than before. The Grants measured selection coefficients equivalent to the strongest ever documented in a natural population, and demonstrated that natural selection operates on generational timescales — not just geological ones.
entries.note: Boag, P. T. & Grant, P. R. (1981). 'Intense natural selection in a population of Darwin's finches (Geospizinae) in the Galápagos.' Science, 214, 82–85. Extended in Grant, P. R. (1986). Ecology and Evolution of Darwin's Finches. Princeton University Press. Selection reversed in the following wet year — the finch beak evolved in both directions across decades in response to climate variation (Grant & Grant 2002 Science). Genetic basis: beak morphology associated with BMP4 and Calmodulin expression (Abzhanov et al. 2004 Science).

entries.label: Bateman: Mating Variance in Drosophila
entries.hint: Bateman crossed Drosophila melanogaster flies bearing different visible mutations to infer mating success from offspring genotypes. He reported that male reproductive success increased steeply with the number of mates while female reproductive success plateaued after a single mating — proposing that variance in mating success is consistently greater in males across animal species. This asymmetry, he argued, explains the near-universal pattern of male competition and female choosiness observed across the animal kingdom.
entries.note: Bateman, A. J. (1948). 'Intra-sexual selection in Drosophila.' Heredity, 2, 349–368. Hugely influential: provided the empirical underpinning for Trivers' parental investment theory (1972) and the entire subsequent field of sexual selection research. Reanalysis: Gowaty, P. A., Kim, Y.-K. & Anderson, W. W. (2012) PNAS found the original data unreliable — mating number was systematically undercounted for females because many offspring lacked informative markers. The Bateman gradient is nonetheless confirmed across 66 species by Janicke et al. (2016) Science Advances, supporting the directional prediction even if Bateman's own data are compromised.

entries.label: Belyayev's Silver Fox Domestication Experiment
entries.loc: Institute of Cytology and Genetics, Akademgorodok, Novosibirsk, Russia
entries.hint: Dmitry Belyayev began selecting silver foxes in 1959 on a single criterion: tolerance of humans. Within 10–15 generations, the tame-selected foxes began showing traits nobody selected for — floppy ears, piebald coats, curled tails, shortened snouts, and dog-like vocalisations. The experiment demonstrated that selecting for a behavioural disposition can rapidly reorganise an animal's developmental programme, producing correlated morphological changes as a byproduct.
entries.note: Belyaev, D. K. (1979). 'Destabilizing selection as a factor in domestication.' Journal of Heredity, 70, 301–308. Trut, L. N. (1999). 'Early Canid Domestication: The Farm-Fox Experiment.' American Scientist, 87, 160–169 — the experiment's definitive popular account. Genetic basis: Kukekova, A. V. et al. (2018). 'Red fox genome assembly identifies genomic regions associated with tame and aggressive behaviours.' Nature Ecology & Evolution, 2, 1479–1491. The experiment has now run for 65+ years across 50+ generations; tame foxes are available as pets in Russia.

categories.label: Effects & Phenomena
categories.subtitle: Named, replicable patterns in animal behaviour and ecology

entries.label: Kin Discrimination
entries.hint: Many animals preferentially direct altruistic behaviour toward genetic relatives, and can recognise them without prior familiarisation — using chemical cues (major histocompatibility complex odour in mammals), acoustic cues (ground squirrel calls), or morphological similarity. Kin discrimination allows Hamilton's rule to operate precisely rather than probabilistically, making conditional altruism more efficient. The mechanism varies by taxon; no single kin-recognition system is universal.
entries.note: Holmes, W. G. & Sherman, P. W. (1982). 'The ontogeny of kin recognition in two species of ground squirrels.' American Zoologist, 22, 491–517. MHC-based kin recognition in mice: Yamazaki et al. (1976) Journal of Experimental Medicine. Review: Penn, D. J. & Frommen, J. G. (2010) in Animal Behaviour: Evolution and Mechanisms (Springer). Cross-taxa meta-analysis confirms kin discrimination in insects, fish, amphibians, birds and mammals.

entries.label: Bateman's Principle
entries.hint: In most sexually reproducing species, male reproductive success increases more steeply with the number of mates than female reproductive success does, because sperm are cheap and eggs are costly. This creates stronger selection on males to maximise mating opportunities and on females to be choosy. The principle predicts the direction of sexual dimorphism, intensity of male-male competition, and degree of female choosiness across species.
entries.note: Bateman, A. J. (1948). 'Intra-sexual selection in Drosophila.' Heredity, 2, 349–368. Reanalysis: Gowaty, P. A., Kim, Y.-K. & Anderson, W. W. (2012). 'No evidence of sexual selection in a repetition of Bateman's classic study of Drosophila melanogaster.' PNAS, 109, 11740–11745 — found the original data are unreliable and the effect disappears with better methods. Counter-reanalysis: Janicke et al. (2016) Science Advances found Bateman gradients across many species, supporting the principle directionally though magnitude varies.

entries.label: Marginal Value Theorem
entries.loc: University of Utah, Salt Lake City, Utah
entries.hint: An optimal forager should leave a depleting food patch when its instantaneous harvest rate drops to match the average rate achievable across the whole habitat (including travel between patches). This predicts that animals leave rich patches later, leave poor patches sooner, and stay longer in all patches as habitat-average quality declines. The theorem has been used to model everything from bee foraging to search engine usage.
entries.note: Charnov, E. L. (1976). 'Optimal foraging: the marginal value theorem.' Theoretical Population Biology, 9, 129–136. Meta-analysis: Nonacs, P. (2001) Behavioral Ecology — 53% of studies show quantitative agreement; deviations tend to occur when predation risk or nutrient balance is relevant. Cowie (1977) tested in great tits with variable inter-patch distances: qualitative support, quantitative overestimates of patch residence time. Humans also approximately follow MVT in information search (Hills et al. 2008 Psychological Review).

entries.label: Species–Area Relationship
entries.hint: The number of species on an island (or in any habitat patch) increases as a power function of area — typically S = cA^z where z is around 0.25. Doubling habitat area adds roughly 20% more species; reducing area by 90% (as in fragmentation) loses roughly 50%. The relationship arises from the dynamic balance between immigration and extinction rates, and has become the empirical backbone of conservation biology's predictions about habitat loss.
entries.note: MacArthur, R. H. & Wilson, E. O. (1967). The Theory of Island Biogeography. Princeton University Press. Empirical test: Simberloff & Wilson (1969) Ecology — defaunated Florida mangrove islands recolonised to predicted equilibria. Applied to continental fragments: meta-analysis Drakare et al. (2006) Ecology Letters — z values range 0.1–0.4 across terrestrial taxa; z is steeper for islands than habitat patches. Limitation: the relationship predicts species numbers but not which species, making it a coarse planning tool.

entries.label: Müllerian Mimicry
entries.hint: Two or more unpalatable species converge on the same warning coloration, each benefiting from the other's learned predator aversion — the more models share a signal, the fewer individuals each species loses as predators learn to avoid the pattern. Unlike Batesian mimicry, both species are genuinely defended, so the system is mutually beneficial. Heliconius butterflies display this convergence dramatically, with co-mimics sharing identical wing patterns across species.
entries.note: Müller, F. (1879). 'Ituna and Thyridia: a remarkable case of mimicry in butterflies.' Transactions of the Entomological Society of London, 20–29. Genetic mechanism in Heliconius: a single locus (optix) controls red pattern elements and repeatedly produces convergence (Reed et al. 2011 Science). Hybrid zone experiments confirm predator selection on wing pattern (Merrill et al. 2012 Evolution). Distinguished from Batesian mimicry by Poulton (1890). Cross-species gene flow at mimicry loci confirmed by whole-genome sequencing (Heliconius Genome Consortium 2012 Nature).

entries.label: Batesian Mimicry
entries.hint: A palatable, defenceless species gains protection by resembling an unpalatable, defended model species. Predators trained on the model avoid the mimic despite its harmlessness. Unlike Müllerian mimicry, the relationship is parasitic: as mimic frequency increases relative to the model, predators encounter more palatable individuals and the protection degrades — the system is frequency-dependent and maintained at low mimic-to-model ratios.
entries.note: Bates, H. W. (1862). 'Contributions to an insect fauna of the Amazon valley.' Transactions of the Linnean Society of London, 23, 495–566. Experimental proof: Brower, L. P. (1958) Evolution — blue-jay aversion to viceroys trained on monarchs. Revisionist finding: Ritland & Brower (1991) Nature showed viceroy butterflies may also be unpalatable (making them co-Müllerian mimics, not Batesian); the classic case is now debated. Frequency-dependence confirmed experimentally by Lindström et al. (1997) Proceedings of the Royal Society B.

entries.label: Brood Parasite Arms Race
entries.hint: Host species of brood parasites (cuckoos, cowbirds, honeyguides) evolve egg rejection and nest desertion; parasites evolve egg mimicry and aggressive host manipulation; hosts then evolve finer discrimination — a coevolutionary arms race visible within and across populations. The intensity of host defences tracks the duration of parasitism exposure, providing a natural experiment in coevolution across a geographic mosaic.
entries.note: Davies, N. B. & Brooke, M. de L. (1989). Journal of Animal Ecology, 58, 207–236. Egg mimicry genetic basis: Fossøy et al. (2016) Nature Genetics. Geographic mosaic confirmed: Soler & Møller (1990) Oecologia — reed warblers in high-cuckoo areas reject more eggs than those in low-cuckoo areas. Cowbird example: Rothstein (1990) Science — rejection behaviour in North American hosts similarly correlated with exposure history. Rapid evolutionary change in host defences within <50 years documented.

entries.label: Bruce Effect
entries.loc: Institute of Animal Genetics, Edinburgh, Scotland
entries.hint: In house mice and several other rodents, exposure of a recently mated female to the scent of an unfamiliar male causes her to spontaneously abort her current pregnancy and become receptive to the new male. The mechanism is chemosensory — specific volatiles in male urine detected via the vomeronasal organ activate neuroendocrine pathways that suppress prolactin, blocking implantation or inducing early foetal resorption. The effect is absent if the new male's scent matches the stud's.
entries.note: Bruce, H. M. (1959). 'An exteroceptive block to pregnancy in the mouse.' Nature, 184, 105. Mechanism: Keverne, E. B. & Rosser, A. E. (1986) Journal of Reproduction and Fertility — vomeronasal input critical. Confirmed in wild mice, deer mice and prairie voles; also reported in primates (Pereira 1983). Adaptive interpretation: females avoid investing in offspring sired by a male who could be killed, shifting investment to offspring of the dominant male. Critique: effect may not occur in wild conditions at ecologically relevant male encounter rates.

entries.label: Dear Enemy Effect
entries.loc: Rockefeller University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Territorial animals show weaker aggression toward familiar neighbours than toward unfamiliar strangers, despite neighbours being equally dangerous as competitors. Once territory boundaries are established, both parties benefit from respecting the status quo — escalated fights are costly and boundaries rarely change. Animals learn individual-specific vocal or chemical signatures and reduce costly responses to established rivals, allocating aggression to genuine new threats.
entries.note: Coined by Fisher, J. (1954) but experimentally established by Temeles, E. J. (1994) Animal Behaviour for a formal review. Original experiments: Krebs (1976) showed great tits respond more strongly to stranger than neighbour song playback. Meta-analysis: Temeles (1994) found the effect in 72% of 44 studies across birds, fish and mammals. Mechanism: individual vocal recognition confirmed using discriminant function analysis of sonograms. The effect is context-dependent — during breeding season early-on, the dear enemy effect can be reversed (lyrebirds, some frogs).

categories.label: Key Concepts
categories.subtitle: The building blocks and vocabulary every student must command

entries.label: Fixed Action Pattern
entries.loc: Max Planck Institute for Behavioural Physiology, Seewiesen, Germany
entries.hint: A Fixed Action Pattern (FAP) is an innate, stereotyped motor sequence that runs to completion once triggered by a specific sign stimulus (releaser), regardless of whether the stimulus continues. A greylag goose rolling a displaced egg back to the nest continues the rolling motion even if the egg is removed mid-sequence. FAPs represent the most basic unit of innate behaviour and were central to the ethological programme of cataloguing species-typical actions — though modern research recognises that most such sequences can be modulated by context or internal state (hunger, arousal, prior stimulation), and Barlow (1977) proposed the term 'modal action pattern' to better reflect this variability.
entries.note: Coined by Lorenz, K. & Tinbergen, N. (1938). 'Taxis und Instinkthandlung in der Eirollbewegung der Graugans.' Zeitschrift für Tierpsychologie, 2, 1–29. Revised to 'modal action pattern' by Barlow (1977) to acknowledge intraspecific variability. The concept remains useful for describing stereotyped reflexes, but pure FAPs are now seen as endpoints of a continuum from reflexes to highly flexible behaviours; most behaviour shows more variability than early ethologists acknowledged.

entries.label: Imprinting
entries.hint: Imprinting is irreversible learning during a developmentally restricted sensitive period that shapes a lasting preference or bond. Filial imprinting determines the species the young animal treats as parent; sexual imprinting determines the species characteristics sought in a future mate. Unlike most learning, imprinted preferences are formed before the reinforcing outcome (mating) occurs, and once consolidated they are difficult or impossible to reverse — a developmental canalization of social preferences.
entries.note: Lorenz, K. (1935). Der Kumpan in der Umwelt des Vogels. Journal für Ornithologie, 83. 'Sensitive period' replaces Lorenz's 'critical period' in modern usage — Sluckin, W. (1965) Imprinting and Early Learning. Sexual imprinting: Immelmann, K. (1972) Advances in the Study of Behavior, 4, 147–174. Mechanism: the sensitive period is regulated by GABA neurotransmission and experience-dependent CREB phosphorylation in the IMHV/intermediate medial mesopallium (Horn 2004 Nature Reviews Neuroscience).

entries.label: Eusociality
entries.hint: Eusociality is the most extreme form of social organisation: overlapping generations, cooperative brood care, and reproductive division of labour with a reproductively dominant caste and sterile or sub-fertile workers. It has evolved independently in Hymenoptera (ants, bees, wasps), termites, shrimp and one mammal (the naked mole-rat). Kin selection explains Hymenoptera eusociality through the peculiarity of haplodiploidy — sisters share 75% of genes — but the naked mole-rat refutes haplodiploid necessity.
entries.note: Term coined by Batra, S. (1966) Insectes Sociaux. Criterion formalised by Wilson, E. O. (1971). The Insect Societies. Belknap Press. Hamilton (1964) provided the kin-selection explanation for Hymenoptera. Naked mole-rat eusociality: Jarvis, J. U. M. (1981) Science — workers in a subterranean colony with a single breeding queen. Challenge to kin selection: Nowak, Tarnita & Wilson (2010) Nature argued eusociality requires ecological pre-adaptation rather than high relatedness — strongly rebutted by 130+ co-signatories.

entries.label: Honest vs Arbitrary Signalling
entries.loc: Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Jerusalem, Israel
entries.hint: Honest signals fall into two types: handicaps (energetically costly, so only high-quality individuals can afford them without net loss — peacock tails, elaborate birdsong) and index signals (structurally constrained, physically impossible to fake — a deep frog croak that directly reflects body size). A third type — conventional signals — carries meaning by shared agreement rather than cost, and persists through social enforcement rather than physical constraint. Arbitrary cheap signals cannot spread where signaller and receiver interests conflict, because low-quality signalers would invade; where interests align (coordinating mates within a pair), cheap convention can be stable.
entries.note: Zahavi, A. (1975) introduced the honest signalling via cost argument. Formal models: Grafen, A. (1990) Journal of Theoretical Biology; Maynard Smith & Harper (2003) Animal Signals — distinguished index signals (morphological, cannot be faked), handicaps (energetically costly), and conventional signals. Empirical test of convention: Tibbetts & Dale (2004) Nature — wasp facial patterns maintain convention by mutual non-escalation, not cost. Review: Searcy & Nowicki (2005) The Evolution of Animal Communication. Princeton University Press.

entries.label: Ecological Niche
entries.hint: An ecological niche is the multidimensional hypervolume of environmental conditions and resource axes within which a species can persist and reproduce — its Hutchinsonian niche. The realised niche is narrower than the fundamental niche because of competition with other species. The concept explains character displacement (competing species diverge in morphology to partition resources), niche conservatism (closely related species resist ecological change), and why no two species occupy exactly the same niche in stable communities.
entries.note: Grinnell, J. (1917) originated the habitat niche. Formal multidimensional definition: Hutchinson, G. E. (1957). 'Concluding remarks.' Cold Spring Harbor Symposia on Quantitative Biology, 22, 415–427. Competitive exclusion principle: Gause, G. F. (1934) The Struggle for Existence. Character displacement: Brown & Wilson (1956). Niche conservatism review: Wiens & Graham (2005) Annual Review of Ecology, Evolution and Systematics. The niche concept has been operationalised in species distribution modelling (MaxEnt).

entries.label: Referential vs Affective Signalling
entries.hint: Animal vocalisations can convey information about external objects or events (referential signals — vervet alarm calls specifying predator class) or about the internal state of the signaller (affective signals — fear calls indicating arousal level regardless of cause). The distinction maps loosely onto human language's capacity for semantic reference. Most animal signals are primarily affective; a minority show genuine referential specificity that goes beyond general alarm or arousal.
entries.note: Distinction introduced by Marler, P. (1961). 'The filtering of external stimuli during instinctive behaviour.' In Thorpe & Zangwill (Eds.), Current Problems in Animal Behaviour. Cambridge University Press. Functionally referential calls formally defined: Macedonia & Evans (1993) Ethology. Survey of evidence: Zuberbühler, K. (2009) in The Oxford Handbook of Language Evolution — referential specificity confirmed in primates, corvids, and some rodents, but the degree to which animals encode predicate vs. object information remains debated.

entries.label: Coefficient of Relatedness
entries.hint: The coefficient of relatedness (r) is the probability that an allele in one individual is identical by descent to an allele at the same locus in another individual. Full siblings and parent-offspring pairs share r = 0.5; half-siblings r = 0.25; first cousins r = 0.125. Hamilton's rule rB > C uses r to weight how much an actor should value a relative's welfare versus its own — r is the genetic currency of altruism, predicting which family relationships should elicit help and how much.
entries.note: Hamilton, W. D. (1964). Journal of Theoretical Biology, 7, 1–52. Wright, S. (1922) developed the coefficient of kinship as a population genetics concept; Hamilton adapted it to social evolution. Measurement in the field: microsatellite genotyping now allows empirical r estimation in wild populations (Queller & Goodnight 1989 Evolution). Key nuance: r must be measured relative to the population average (regression r), not just pedigree expectation — this was long confused in the literature.

entries.label: Behavioural Syndromes & Animal Personality
entries.hint: Individuals within a species consistently differ in suites of correlated behaviours across contexts — a bold individual tends to be more aggressive, more exploratory and more active across situations, not just in one. These correlated behaviour packages are called behavioural syndromes (the population-level pattern) or animal personalities (the individual-level description). The correlations mean that selection on one trait (boldness in predator escape) carries along others (aggressiveness in competition), constraining how independently each behaviour can evolve and explaining why seemingly maladaptive trait combinations persist within populations.
entries.note: Sih, A., Bell, A. M., Johnson, J. C. & Ziemba, R. E. (2004). 'Behavioral syndromes: an integrative overview.' Quarterly Review of Biology, 79, 241–277. Individual consistency: Réale, D. et al. (2007) Philosophical Transactions B — defined five personality axes (bold/shy, exploration, activity, sociability, aggressiveness) with cross-taxa evidence. Genetic basis: narrow-sense heritabilities for boldness typically 0.2–0.5 (Dingemanse & Réale 2005 Behavioral Ecology). Critique: Bell (2007) Animal Behaviour — many studies confound true cross-context correlations with measurement artefacts; repeatability estimates vary widely by method. Fitness consequences: bold great tits have higher reproductive success in novel environments but higher predation risk (Quinn et al. 2011 Proceedings of the Royal Society B).

entries.label: Domestication Syndrome
entries.loc: Institute of Cytology and Genetics, Akademgorodok, Novosibirsk, Russia
entries.hint: Across independently domesticated species — dogs, cats, pigs, horses, cattle, rabbits — the same cluster of traits reliably co-occurs: floppy ears, piebald coat patches, shortened snouts, smaller teeth, reduced adrenal glands, neoteny (juvenile retention of features), and increased docility. Because these traits were never directly selected together, their co-occurrence implies a shared developmental mechanism. The leading explanation is that selection for reduced fear reduces neural crest cell populations, whose derivatives include adrenal tissue, pigmentation, facial bones, and ear cartilage.
entries.note: Belyaev, D. K. (1979). 'Destabilizing selection as a factor in domestication.' Journal of Heredity, 70, 301–308 — first systematic account of correlated trait changes from selection on behaviour. Neural crest hypothesis: Wilkins, A. S., Wrangham, R. W. & Fitch, W. T. (2014). 'The Domestication Syndrome in Mammals: A Unified Explanation Based on Neural Crest Cell Behavior and Genetics.' Genetics, 197, 795–808. Critique: Sánchez-Villagra, M. R. et al. (2016) argue the syndrome is overstated and that trait co-occurrence varies substantially across domesticated lineages.

categories.label: Methods & Approaches
categories.subtitle: The tools and paradigms that generate the field's evidence

entries.label: Ethogram & Focal Animal Sampling
entries.hint: An ethogram is an exhaustive catalogue of all distinct behaviours in a species' repertoire, each defined operationally so that independent observers agree on when each behaviour occurs. Focal animal sampling records all behaviours of a single individual for a fixed period before switching to another — generating unbiased time budgets. Together, these are the foundational methods of field ethology: they prevent selective observation, enable cross-study comparison, and can reveal the full behavioural complexity of a species.
entries.note: Formalised by Tinbergen, N. (1963) and Altmann, J. (1974). 'Observational study of behavior: sampling methods.' Behaviour, 49, 227–267. Altmann's paper established the sampling method hierarchy (ad libitum → focal animal → scan) and documented the biases of each. Now standardised in the ETHOSYS framework. Ethograms are an entry-point for computational behaviour analysis — automatic ethogram scoring from video using machine learning (DeepLabCut; SLEAP) is a rapidly growing area.

entries.label: Radio Telemetry & Satellite Tracking
entries.hint: Attaching a transmitter to an animal allows continuous or periodic location tracking, revealing home ranges, migration routes, daily movement patterns and habitat use. VHF radio tags require manual triangulation; modern GPS and Argos satellite tags upload coordinates automatically at fine temporal resolution. The technology transformed our understanding of migration (albatross circumnavigate the Southern Ocean), predator territory size, and the importance of specific stopover sites for migratory birds.
entries.note: Cochran, W. W. & Lord, R. D. (1963). 'A radio-tracking system for wild animals.' Journal of Wildlife Management, 27, 9–24. Satellite tracking: Service Argos operational from 1978. GPS precision tracking of albatrosses: Weimerskirch et al. (2002) Nature. Miniaturisation milestones: geolocators tracking songbirds across hemispheres (Stutchbury et al. 2009 Science). Methodological limitation: battery life vs. sampling interval trade-off; attachment method can alter behaviour and survival (Geen et al. 2019 Methods in Ecology and Evolution).

entries.label: Playback Experiments
entries.hint: Broadcasting a pre-recorded acoustic signal to a subject and measuring its behavioural response allows controlled manipulation of signal parameters (frequency, duration, caller identity) while keeping all else constant. Playback revealed the semantic content of vervet alarm calls, confirmed that great tits recognise neighbour songs, and demonstrated that frogs discriminate conspecific advertisement calls from heterospecific ones. Stimuli can be synthesised or modified to test which acoustic features drive responses.
entries.note: Early uses: Marler, P. & Tamura, M. (1964) Science — song dialect learning in white-crowned sparrows. Standardised methodology: McGregor, P. K. (Ed.) (1992). Playback and Studies of Animal Communication. Plenum Press. Pitfall: habituation to repeated playback inflates effect sizes in first-trial designs; asymmetric designs recommended (McGregor & Krebs 1984). Synthetic stimuli: Bradbury & Vehrencamp (1998) Principles of Animal Communication. Now combined with audio spectrogram analysis (Raven Pro software).

entries.label: Mark-Recapture Population Estimation
entries.hint: A sample of animals is captured, individually marked and released; a second sample is later taken and the proportion of marked individuals among the recaptured animals estimates total population size (Lincoln-Petersen: N = (M × C) / R). Repeated sessions with multi-state models (Cormack-Jolly-Seber) estimate survival rates, immigration and emigration simultaneously. Mark-recapture is the standard method for estimating wildlife population size and trend when direct census is impossible.
entries.note: Petersen, C. G. J. (1896) invented the method for fish. Lincoln, F. C. (1930) applied it to waterfowl. Jolly-Seber open-population models: Jolly (1965) Biometrika; Seber (1965) Biometrika. Modern software: Program MARK (White & Burnham 1999). Assumption violations (non-random capture, mark loss, non-closure) can bias estimates; heterogeneity in capture probability is the most serious problem. DNA mark-recapture using non-invasive samples (faeces, hair) avoids the need to capture animals (Waits & Paetkau 2005 Journal of Wildlife Management).

entries.label: Phylogenetic Comparative Methods
entries.hint: Species cannot be treated as statistically independent data points because closely related species share ancestral traits by common descent — violations of statistical independence inflate Type I error. Phylogenetic comparative methods (PCMs) control for this shared history by fitting trait evolution on a phylogenetic tree. They test whether traits co-evolve across lineages, estimate ancestral states, and partition phenotypic variance into phylogenetic and non-phylogenetic components.
entries.note: Felsenstein, J. (1985). 'Phylogenies and the comparative method.' American Naturalist, 125, 1–15 — introduced phylogenetic independent contrasts (PICs). Expanded by Harvey, P. H. & Pagel, M. D. (1991). The Comparative Method in Evolutionary Biology. Oxford University Press. Bayesian PCMs: BayesTraits software (Pagel & Meade 2006). Modern R packages: ape, phytools, geiger. Limitation: results are sensitive to phylogenetic uncertainty; multi-tree analyses or Bayesian approaches are now standard.

entries.label: Evolutionary Game Theory Modelling
entries.hint: Game-theoretic models assign payoff matrices to behavioural strategies and find the evolutionarily stable equilibrium — the frequency at which no mutant strategy can invade. Models can be analytical (solve for the ESS algebraically) or simulation-based (agent-based models with replicator dynamics). The approach generates precise, testable predictions about the frequency of alternative mating tactics, fighting escalation rates, and territorial contest outcomes.
entries.note: Maynard Smith, J. & Price, G. R. (1973) Nature — original hawk-dove game. Textbook: Maynard Smith, J. (1982). Evolution and the Theory of Games. Cambridge University Press. Agent-based extensions: Axelrod (1984). Replicator dynamics formalised: Taylor & Jonker (1978) Mathematical Biosciences. Modern evolutionary game theory merges with network science to model spatial and structured populations (Nowak & May 1992 Nature). Software: EGT Simulator; NetLogo.

categories.label: Pioneers & Theorists
categories.subtitle: The researchers who shaped the discipline

entries.label: Konrad Lorenz
entries.hint: Lorenz established ethology as the scientific study of innate behaviour, demonstrating that animals possess a repertoire of species-specific Fixed Action Patterns released by specific stimuli — instincts that are as subject to natural selection as morphology. His work on imprinting, aggression (On Aggression, 1963) and the concept of the Innate Releasing Mechanism created the conceptual vocabulary for the field. He shared the 1973 Nobel Prize with Tinbergen and von Frisch.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1973. Key works: Lorenz, K. (1935) Der Kumpan; Lorenz, K. (1950) 'The comparative method in studying innate behaviour patterns.' Symposia of the Society for Experimental Biology, 4, 221–268. Controversy: Lorenz's wartime writings expressed Nazi ideology and scientific racism; his 1940 paper on domestication advocated eugenic policies. Reviewed in Deichmann, U. (1992) Biologen unter Hitler and by Kalikow (1983) Journal of the History of Biology. Scientific legacy unaffected but biographical canonisation is contested.

entries.label: Nikolaas Tinbergen
entries.hint: Tinbergen built ethology's empirical and conceptual scaffolding: the study of instinct (1951), the four questions framework (1963), and the experimental analysis of sign stimuli using dummy models. He systematically demonstrated that behaviour could be analysed with the same rigour as morphology — designing field experiments to isolate causal variables. He also co-founded behavioural ecology and mentored the generation that extended ethology into evolutionary theory.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1973. Key works: Tinbergen, N. (1951). The Study of Instinct. Clarendon Press, Oxford; Tinbergen, N. (1963). 'On aims and methods of ethology.' Zeitschrift für Tierpsychologie, 20, 410–433. Earlier career at Leiden University before moving to Oxford in 1949. In later years (1970s) focused on ethological analysis of autism (with his wife Elisabeth Tinbergen) — now considered methodologically unsound and harmful.

entries.label: Karl von Frisch
entries.hint: Von Frisch spent three decades deciphering the symbolic communication system of honeybees, revealing that the waggle dance encodes direction and distance of food sources relative to the sun — the first demonstration of non-human symbolic, displaced reference. He also discovered that fish possess a chemical alarm system and that bees have colour and UV vision. His patient, quantitative field experiments defined the ethological ideal of careful observation before inference.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1973. Key works: von Frisch, K. (1967). The Dance Language and Orientation of Bees. Belknap Press. Early career at University of Rostock; most work at Munich. During the Nazi period, his Jewish ancestry put him in danger; his bee research was considered 'essential to agriculture' and he was protected. Wenner, A. M. (1971) The Bee Language Controversy contested the dance-language interpretation; refuted by Gould (1975) Science.

entries.label: Edward O. Wilson
entries.hint: Wilson synthesised evolutionary biology, genetics and ecology into sociobiology — the systematic study of the biological basis of all social behaviour from insects to humans. His monumental Sociobiology (1975) catalogued social behaviour across taxa and explicitly applied the framework to Homo sapiens, triggering the most intense scientific and political controversy in 20th-century biology. His work on island biogeography, ant ecology and biodiversity conservation transformed multiple fields simultaneously.
entries.note: Key works: Wilson, E. O. (1975). Sociobiology: The New Synthesis. Belknap Press; MacArthur, R. H. & Wilson, E. O. (1967). The Theory of Island Biogeography. Princeton University Press; Wilson, E. O. (1998). Consilience. Knopf. Controversy: Allen et al. (1975) in a New York Review of Books letter signed by 15 scientists accused Sociobiology of biological determinism. The final chapter on human behaviour was the primary target. Wilson later contributed to multi-level selection controversies: Nowak, Tarnita & Wilson (2010) Nature.

entries.label: William D. Hamilton
entries.hint: Hamilton solved the central puzzle of evolutionary biology — why should an individual sacrifice its own reproduction for others — by extending natural selection to operate on shared genes rather than individuals. His 1964 papers on inclusive fitness and Hamilton's rule are among the most cited in biology. He also contributed the geometric sex ratio theory, the parasite theory of sex, and collaborated on the Axelrod tournament demonstrating the evolution of cooperation.
entries.note: Key works: Hamilton, W. D. (1964). 'The genetical evolution of social behaviour I & II.' Journal of Theoretical Biology, 7, 1–52; Hamilton, W. D. (1967). 'Extraordinary sex ratios.' Science, 156, 477–488; Hamilton, W. D. et al. (1990) PNAS — parasites and sex. Maynard Smith coined 'kin selection' for Hamilton's mechanism. Dawkins popularised Hamilton's ideas in The Selfish Gene (1976). Hamilton died in 2000 from complications following a Congo field trip; he held an unconventional theory of the origins of HIV.

entries.label: Robert Trivers
entries.hint: Trivers produced a remarkable string of theoretical contributions between 1971 and 1974: reciprocal altruism (1971), parental investment theory (1972), parent-offspring conflict (1974) and sex ratio manipulation (1974). His parental investment theory grounded the logic of sexual selection in the economics of investment, unifying Darwin's observations with population genetics. His work on parent-offspring conflict — that even full siblings are predicted to compete over parental resources — was initially controversial and is now foundational.
entries.note: Key works: Trivers (1971) Quarterly Review of Biology; Trivers (1972) in Campbell (Ed.); Trivers, R. L. (1974). 'Parent-offspring conflict.' American Zoologist, 14, 249–264. Crafoord Prize 2007. Autobiography: Trivers, R. (2015) Wild Life. Sociobiology and the emergence of evolutionary psychology owe much to Trivers. His collaboration with Hamilton on the Axelrod tournament extended reciprocal altruism. Later career marked by controversies including a defamation dispute with Rutgers University.

entries.label: John Maynard Smith
entries.hint: Maynard Smith imported game theory into evolutionary biology to explain why animal conflicts are often ritualised rather than escalated to lethal combat. His concept of the evolutionarily stable strategy (ESS) — a strategy that, once common, cannot be invaded by mutants — replaced group-selection explanations with individual-level evolutionary logic. He also clarified units of selection, contributed to the evolution of sex theory, and synthesised evolutionary genetics and developmental biology.
entries.note: Key works: Maynard Smith, J. & Price, G. R. (1973) Nature; Maynard Smith, J. (1982). Evolution and the Theory of Games. Cambridge University Press; Maynard Smith, J. & Szathmáry, E. (1995). The Major Transitions in Evolution. Oxford University Press. Crafoord Prize 1999. Early career as an aeronautical engineer (Hawker Aircraft) before returning to biology. Was a student of Haldane. The ESS framework has been extended far beyond animal conflicts into economics, immunology and cancer biology.

entries.label: Jane Goodall
entries.hint: Goodall's decades-long study of chimpanzees at Gombe overturned assumptions about human uniqueness. She documented tool use (stripping leaves from twigs to fish termites from mounds), cooperative hunting, inter-group warfare, infanticide, and complex social hierarchies — behaviours then believed exclusively human. By habituating wild chimps to human presence and following named individuals across lifetimes, she established the long-term field study as the gold standard for primate behaviour.
entries.note: Key works: Goodall, J. (1963). 'Feeding behaviour of wild chimpanzees.' Symposia of the Zoological Society of London, 10, 39–47; Goodall, J. (1986). The Chimpanzees of Gombe. Belknap Press. Affiliated with Louis Leakey (National Museums of Kenya) and later Cambridge. Discovery of chimpanzee tool use prompted Leakey's famous remark: 'Now we must redefine tool, redefine man, or accept chimpanzees as humans.' United Nations Messenger of Peace since 2002. Methodological critique: naming and habituating subjects risks anthropomorphism.

entries.label: Richard Dawkins
entries.hint: Dawkins reframed evolutionary theory from the organism's perspective to the gene's: genes are the replicators that persist across generations, and bodies are their temporary vehicles. The Selfish Gene (1976) popularised Hamilton's inclusive fitness, Maynard Smith's ESS and Trivers' theories for a general audience and introduced the concept of the meme. Extended Phenotype (1982) argued that genes manipulate not just their own organism but also the environment and other organisms.
entries.note: Key works: Dawkins, R. (1976). The Selfish Gene. Oxford University Press; Dawkins, R. (1982). The Extended Phenotype. Oxford University Press. The gene-centred view synthesised Fisher, Hamilton and Williams rather than originating new biology. Controversy: Gould & Lewontin (1979) and Gould (2002) critiqued 'genocentrism' and adaptionism. Nowak et al. (2010) Nature disputed the exclusive sufficiency of inclusive fitness. Dawkins' later public profile (atheism advocacy) is separate from his scientific legacy.

entries.label: Ernst Mayr
entries.loc: American Museum of Natural History, New York, New York
entries.hint: Mayr provided the biological species concept — species are groups of actually or potentially interbreeding natural populations reproductively isolated from other such groups — and the geographic theory of speciation: new species form primarily through allopatry (geographic isolation followed by divergence). These two contributions unified evolutionary biology and systematics, allowing the modern synthesis to incorporate natural history collections. He also formalised the proximate/ultimate distinction that Tinbergen later extended.
entries.note: Key works: Mayr, E. (1942). Systematics and the Origin of Species. Columbia University Press; Mayr, E. (1963). Animal Species and Evolution. Belknap Press. Biological species concept: challenged by Mallet (1995) Trends in Ecology & Evolution — many species hybridise; phylogenetic and cohesion species concepts proposed as alternatives. The BSC remains standard in zoology. Mayr contributed the proximate/ultimate distinction in Mayr (1961) Science, 134, 1501–1506, predating Tinbergen's four questions.

entries.label: Donald Griffin
entries.hint: Griffin discovered echolocation in bats — that they emit ultrasonic pulses and use the returning echoes to navigate and detect prey — and named the phenomenon 'biosonar.' He later founded cognitive ethology, arguing that animal minds — consciousness, imagery, intention — were legitimate subjects of scientific inquiry, directly challenging the behaviourist taboo on mentalism. The Question of Animal Awareness (1976) provoked a paradigm shift toward taking animal subjective experience seriously.
entries.note: Key works: Griffin, D. R. (1944). 'Echolocation by blind men, bats, and radar.' Science, 100, 589–590; Griffin, D. R. (1958). Listening in the Dark. Yale University Press; Griffin, D. R. (1976). The Question of Animal Awareness. Rockefeller University Press. Echolocation mechanism: Griffin, Galambos & Suga worked out the neural processing. Cognitive ethology critics (Kennedy 1992 The New Anthropomorphism) argued Griffin was anthropomorphising. Modern neuroscience of bat navigation: Moser et al. 2015 Nobel lecture context on grid/place cells in bats.

entries.label: Frans de Waal
entries.loc: Arnhem Zoo / Emory University, Atlanta, Georgia
entries.hint: De Waal documented political alliance formation, reconciliation after conflict, food sharing and empathy-like behaviour in great apes and other primates, arguing that morality and prosociality have deep evolutionary precursors in non-human animals. His study of Machiavellian intelligence in chimpanzees — strategic alliance formation among adult males to gain and maintain alpha status — showed that primate politics involves long-term strategising rather than simple dominance hierarchies.
entries.note: Key works: de Waal, F. B. M. (1982). Chimpanzee Politics. Jonathan Cape; de Waal, F. B. M. (1989). Peacemaking Among Primates. Harvard University Press; de Waal, F. B. M. (2016). Are We Smart Enough to Know How Smart Animals Are? Norton. Empathy evidence: Preston & de Waal (2002) Behavioral and Brain Sciences reviewed the 'perception-action model' of empathy across species. Critique: Povinelli & Vonk (2004) Trends in Cognitive Sciences argued chimpanzees understand behavioural but not mental states — the 'other minds' debate.

entries.label: Dmitry Belyayev & Lyudmila Trut
entries.loc: Institute of Cytology and Genetics, Akademgorodok, Novosibirsk, Russia
entries.hint: Belyayev designed and launched the fox experiment in 1959, framing it as a test of domestication via destabilising selection — a concept politically dangerous in Soviet-era biology still haunted by Lysenkoism. Trut joined as a young researcher in 1960 and became the experiment's scientific lead and public voice after Belyayev's death in 1985, running it continuously for over six decades and co-authoring the 2018 genome paper that finally mapped the genetic basis of tameness.
entries.note: Belyaev, D. K. (1979). Journal of Heredity, 70, 301–308. Trut, L. N. (1999). American Scientist, 87, 160–169. Trut, L., Oskina, I. & Kharlamova, A. (2009). 'Animal evolution during domestication: the domesticated fox as a model.' BioEssays, 31, 349–360. Trut co-authored Kukekova et al. (2018). The experiment operated covertly under the cover of a fur-farm programme during Khrushchev-era science politics; Belyayev served as deputy director of ICG RAS until his death. Trut received the Vavilov Medal in 2010 for the work.

categories.label: Debates & Open Questions
categories.subtitle: Live controversies — where the field is actively contesting the evidence

entries.label: Group Selection vs Kin Selection
entries.loc: University of California, San Diego, California
entries.hint: Wynne-Edwards (1962) argued that animals regulate their population density below resource limits for the 'good of the group' through voluntary reproductive restraint — a group selection argument. Williams (1966) systematically dismantled this, showing that individual selection always overrides group selection when interests conflict because cheater variants (individuals who reproduce at the expense of the group) will always invade. The debate resurfaced with Nowak, Tarnita & Wilson (2010) arguing multi-level selection can explain eusociality without inclusive fitness.
entries.note: Wynne-Edwards, V. C. (1962). Animal Dispersion in Relation to Social Behaviour. Oliver & Boyd. Williams, G. C. (1966). Adaptation and Natural Selection. Princeton University Press. Nowak, M. A., Tarnita, C. E. & Wilson, E. O. (2010). 'The evolution of eusociality.' Nature, 466, 1057–1062 — 137-author rebuttal: Abbot et al. (2011) Nature, 471, E1–E10. Current status: most theorists accept that inclusive fitness and multi-level selection are mathematically equivalent formalisms (Marshall 2011 Journal of Evolutionary Biology), making the debate partly semantic.

entries.label: The Sociobiology Controversy
entries.hint: The publication of Wilson's Sociobiology (1975), especially its final chapter applying evolutionary biology to human behaviour, triggered intense political opposition from within biology itself. Critics argued that biological determinism naturalises inequality, provides pseudo-scientific legitimacy for sexism and racism, and ignores cultural and developmental plasticity. Defenders argued that evolutionary analysis of behaviour is scientifically legitimate and that the political critique confused the naturalistic fallacy with scientific error.
entries.note: Wilson, E. O. (1975). Sociobiology. Belknap Press. Allen, E., et al. (1975). 'Against sociobiology.' New York Review of Books, 22(18). Gould, S. J. & Lewontin, R. C. (1979). 'The Spandrels of San Marco.' Royal Society. Rejoinder: Wilson, E. O. & Harris, M. (1978) various publications. The critique split into two strands: (1) scientific — adaptionism without adequate mechanistic evidence; (2) political — misuse of science. The scientific strand is more credible; evolutionary psychology emerged partly from the debate as a field attempting more rigorous standards.

entries.label: Units of Selection: Gene, Organism or Group?
entries.hint: Does natural selection act primarily on genes (Hamilton, Williams, Dawkins), on individual organisms (most evolutionary biologists), or also on groups (Wilson, Sober, Wynne-Edwards)? The answer matters because group selection can maintain traits harmful to individuals (altruism, restraint) if group-level variance in fitness is large enough. Most modern theorists accept that inclusive fitness and multi-level selection are different bookkeeping methods describing the same underlying process — but this formal equivalence does not end the debate about which level has more explanatory power.
entries.note: Williams, G. C. (1966) against group selection. Dawkins, R. (1976) gene-centred view. Sober, E. & Wilson, D. S. (1998). Unto Others: The Evolution and Psychology of Unselfish Behavior. Harvard University Press — multi-level selection defence. Equivalence proof: Marshall, J. A. R. (2011). 'Group selection and kin selection: formally equivalent approaches.' Trends in Ecology & Evolution, 26, 325–332. Ongoing: Price equation formalism (Frank 1995, 1998) can partition selection at any level, making it level-neutral.

entries.label: Animal Consciousness & Sentience
entries.loc: Rockefeller University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Do animals have subjective experience — is there 'something it is like' to be a bat, a crow, an octopus? The behaviourist tradition avoided the question as scientifically unanswerable; cognitive ethology (Griffin 1976) argued we should at least attempt to study it. The Cambridge Declaration on Consciousness (2012) stated that non-human animals possess the neurological substrates for conscious states. But the hard problem of consciousness remains unsolved, and inferring subjective experience from behaviour or neural correlates is philosophically contested.
entries.note: Griffin, D. R. (1976). The Question of Animal Awareness. Rockefeller University Press. Cambridge Declaration: Low, P. et al. (2012). Cambridge Declaration on Consciousness — signed at Francis Crick Memorial Conference. Critics: Kennedy, J. S. (1992). The New Anthropomorphism. Cambridge University Press. Octopus sentience: Mather, J. A. (2008) Applied Animal Behaviour Science. Fish sentience debate: Sneddon et al. (2018) Philosophical Transactions B vs. Rose et al. (2014) Fish and Fisheries. UK Animal Welfare Act 2022 extended sentience protections to decapod crustaceans.

entries.label: Bateman's Principle Contested
entries.hint: Gowaty et al. (2012) reanalysed Bateman's original Drosophila data and concluded that the data were too poor to support the conclusions: mating number was inferred from offspring genotypes with a method that systematically undercounts female matings, inflating the apparent male advantage in reproductive variance. The finding challenges a central empirical pillar of sexual selection theory — though the Bateman gradient (steeper variance in male than female mating success) has since been confirmed with better methods across many taxa.
entries.note: Gowaty, P. A., Kim, Y.-K. & Anderson, W. W. (2012). 'No evidence of sexual selection in a repetition of Bateman's classic study of Drosophila melanogaster.' PNAS, 109, 11740–11745. Bateman gradient across 66 species: Janicke, T. et al. (2016). 'Darwinian sex roles confirmed across the animal kingdom.' Science Advances, 2, e1500983 — supports directional prediction even if original study was flawed. Critique of critique: Jones, A. G. (2009) had already questioned the operational sex ratio as the correct predictor. Tang-Martinez, Z. (2016) Animal Behaviour reviews wider methodological issues in sexual selection research.

entries.label: Honest Signalling vs Conventional Signals
entries.hint: Granting that signals can be reliable via cost, index-constraint or convention (see co4), the open empirical question is: which mechanism actually predominates in nature, and when is each sufficient? The house sparrow status badge is the central battleground: Møller (1987) found that artificially enlarged badges attracted more female attention without triggering increased male aggression — consistent with conventional enforcement — but subsequent studies showed highly variable effects across populations, undermining any clean answer. More broadly, meta-analyses find that costly signals are common in competitive contexts (high conflict of interest), while low-cost conventional signals cluster in cooperative or low-conflict contexts. Számadó (2011) formalised this: cost is necessary for honesty only proportional to the degree of conflict between signaller and receiver.
entries.note: Zahavi, A. (1975) Nature. Grafen, A. (1990) formalised the cost argument. Maynard Smith, J. & Harper, D. G. C. (2003). Animal Signals. Oxford University Press — distinguished index, handicap and conventional. Wasp facial patterns: Tibbetts, E. A. & Dale, J. (2004) Nature — conventional signals enforced by aggression. Sparrow badge: Møller (1987) Animal Behaviour vs. Griffith et al. (1999) — effects vary by population. Review: Számadó, S. (2011) Animal Behaviour — the cost requirement depends on the degree of conflict of interest between signaller and receiver.

entries.label: Instinct vs Learning: The Blank Slate Debate
entries.hint: Early ethologists (Lorenz, Tinbergen) stressed innate, species-typical behaviour; comparative psychologists (Lehrman, Beach) argued that apparent instincts depend on developmental experience and cannot be cleanly separated from learning. Lehrman's 1953 critique demolished the idea that behaviour can be divided into 'innate' and 'learned' components — all development involves gene-environment interaction. The debate resurfaced in evolutionary psychology's claims about universal human psychological modules.
entries.note: Lehrman, D. S. (1953). 'A critique of Konrad Lorenz's theory of instinctive behavior.' Quarterly Review of Biology, 28, 337–363. Ethology's response: Tinbergen (1963) partially conceded Lehrman's developmental point. Resolution: Bateson, P. & Mameli, M. (2007) Biological Theory — the innate/learned distinction is a family of distinct questions that must be separated. Evolutionary psychology blank-slate critique: Pinker, S. (2002) The Blank Slate vs Buller, D. (2005) Adapting Minds — adaptationist claims about universal psychological modules remain contested.

entries.label: Culture in Non-Human Animals
entries.hint: Whiten et al. (1999) documented 39 behaviours that varied between chimpanzee communities in ways that could not be explained by ecology or genetics alone — defining them as cultural traditions. The finding was controversial: critics argued that social learning, imitation, and 'culture' require definitions clarifying what distinguishes animal traditions from human culture. Subsequent evidence for cultural transmission has accumulated in cetaceans (song dialects in humpback whales) and corvids (tool design variation), but the mechanisms and cognitive requirements remain debated.
entries.note: Whiten, A. et al. (1999). 'Cultures in chimpanzees.' Nature, 399, 682–685. Critics: Laland, K. N. & Janik, V. M. (2006). 'The animal cultures debate.' Trends in Ecology & Evolution, 21, 542–547 — argued evidence for true imitation (vs stimulus enhancement or local enhancement) is thin. Whale song culture: Garland et al. (2011) Current Biology — revolutionary songs spread across populations. Crow tool culture: Hunt & Gray (2003) Animal Cognition. Review: Whiten, A. (2021) Science — multi-species evidence now strong for behavioural traditions; the debate has shifted to whether cultural evolution in animals requires high-fidelity copying.

entries.label: Sexual Conflict
entries.hint: Sexual conflict arises when the evolutionary interests of males and females diverge — what maximises male fitness imposes costs on females, and vice versa. Intralocus conflict: the same allele has different optimal values in males and females (e.g., a gene that increases testosterone boosts male fitness but reduces female fitness when inherited by daughters). Interlocus conflict: male and female genes at different loci co-evolve antagonistically — male seminal proteins that manipulate female re-mating rate, female resistance proteins that counteract them, and the resulting arms race between the sexes. Sexual conflict predicts harmful mating interactions, sex-specific gene expression, and rapid co-evolution of male and female reproductive proteins.
entries.note: Parker, G. A. (1979). 'Sexual selection and sexual conflict.' In Blum, M. S. & Blum, N. A. (Eds.), Sexual Selection and Reproductive Competition in Insects. Academic Press, 123–166. Seminal fluid arms race in Drosophila: Rice, W. R. (1996). 'Sexually antagonistic male adaptation triggered by experimental arrest of female evolution.' Nature, 381, 232–234 — experimental evolution confirmed rapid antagonistic co-evolution. Review: Chapman, T., Arnqvist, G., Bangham, J. & Rowe, L. (2003). 'Sexual conflict.' Trends in Ecology & Evolution, 18, 41–47. Book: Arnqvist, G. & Rowe, L. (2005). Sexual Conflict. Princeton University Press.
