Debate: whether sexual conflict is a dominant force in evolution or a minor modifier of selection — Roughgarden et al. (2006) Science proposed cooperative 'social selection' as an alternative framework.

categories.label: Applications
categories.subtitle: Where the science leaves the lab

entries.label: Conservation Genetics & Population Management
entries.hint: Molecular genetics allows wildlife managers to estimate effective population size, identify conservation units (distinct population segments that require separate management), detect inbreeding depression, and reconstruct dispersal corridors between fragmented habitats. Conservation genetics informs captive breeding programmes (avoid pairing close relatives), translocation decisions (match source populations genetically to recipients), and monitoring of cryptic species that are impossible to count directly.
entries.note: Avise, J. C. (1996). 'Toward a regional conservation genetics perspective: phylogeography of faunal endemics in Southeastern United States.' In Avise, J. C. & Hamrick, J. L. (Eds.) Conservation Genetics. Chapman & Hall. Landmark application: Florida panther genetic rescue (Hedrick & Kalinowski 2000 Science) — eight Texas pumas introduced to increase heterozygosity; survival of kittens increased. Non-invasive sampling (faeces, hair) DNA methods: Taberlet et al. (1999) Trends in Ecology & Evolution. Software: STRUCTURE, BEAST, eDNA detection.

entries.label: Biological Pest Control
entries.hint: Natural enemies of pest species — parasitoids, predators, pathogens — can be introduced or augmented to suppress pest populations below economic thresholds. Optimal foraging theory informs predictions about where natural enemies search, how patch quality affects their residence time, and how to design landscapes to maintain effective biological control. Pheromone traps exploit communication systems to monitor and disrupt mating in agricultural pests.
entries.note: Classic case: vedalia beetle (Rodolia cardinalis) introduced to California in 1888 to control cottony cushion scale — the first successful biological control programme. Review: Eilenberg, J., Hajek, A. & Lomer, C. (2001). 'Suggestions for unifying the terminology in biological control.' BioControl, 46, 387–400. Pheromone disruption: Cardé, R. T. & Minks, A. K. (1995) Annual Review of Entomology. Risks: non-target effects (native species harmed by generalist biocontrol agents) require careful host-specificity testing (Louda et al. 2003 Conservation Biology).

entries.label: Managed Pollinator Conservation
entries.hint: Understanding bee communication, foraging behaviour and hive thermoregulation is essential for managing honeybee colonies and conserving wild pollinators. Colony collapse disorder (documented from 2006) triggered intensive research into pathogen load (Varroa mites, Nosema, virus combinations), pesticide sublethal effects (neonicotinoids impairing navigation), and nutritional stress. Foraging range estimates from waggle dance decoding now inform the design of flowering crop margins and wildflower corridors.
entries.note: Colony collapse disorder: vanEngelsdorp, D. et al. (2009). 'Colony collapse disorder: a descriptive study.' PLoS ONE, 4, e6481. Neonicotinoid navigation impairment: Henry et al. (2012) Science — GPS-tracked foragers exposed to thiamethoxam showed homing failure. Waggle dance decoding for landscape planning: Couvillon et al. (2014) Current Biology mapped forage quality using GPS-calibrated dance decoding. EU partial neonicotinoid ban (2013, extended 2018) informed by bee navigation and communication research.

entries.label: Animal-Assisted Interventions
entries.hint: Interactions with animals reduce stress hormones, lower blood pressure, and improve mood in clinical settings. Ethological understanding of dog domestication, human-dog attachment (mirroring mother-infant bonding systems via oxytocin), and how animals read human social cues underpins the design of effective animal-assisted therapy programmes. Service dogs trained using operant conditioning assist people with PTSD, epilepsy, diabetes and physical disabilities.
entries.note: Friedmann, E. et al. (1984). 'Animal companions and one-year survival after discharge from a coronary care unit.' Public Health Reports, 95, 307–312 — original pet survival study. Oxytocin mechanism: Odendaal, J. S. J. & Meintjes, R. A. (2003) Veterinary Journal; Nagasawa et al. (2015) Science — gaze-mediated oxytocin loop between dogs and owners. Meta-analysis of animal-assisted therapy: Nimer, J. & Lundahl, B. (2007) Anthrozoös, 21, 225–240 — moderate effects across autism, anxiety and behavioural problems. Methodological issues: most studies lack active control groups.

entries.label: GPS Tracking for Migratory Conservation
entries.hint: Miniaturised GPS data loggers attached to migratory birds, sea turtles and marine mammals have revealed the specific stopover sites and marine foraging areas on which migratory populations depend, enabling targeted habitat protection. The discovery that Arctic terns undertake the longest annual migration of any animal (70,000 km round trip, pole to pole) came from geolocator tags first deployed in 2010. Identifying population-specific flyways directs investment to the bottleneck sites most critical for each population.
entries.note: Arctic tern migration: Egevang, C. et al. (2010). 'Tracking of Arctic terns reveals longest animal migration.' PNAS, 107, 2078–2081. Bottleneck identification for conservation: Gill, J. A. et al. (2001) Science — bar-tailed godwit staging sites. IUCN flyway partnerships now coordinate cross-national habitat protection along major bird flyways. Sea turtle tracking: Luschi, P. et al. (1998) Nature. Whale shark migration: Norman et al. (2017) PeerJ. Challenge: tags affect behaviour and survival when too large relative to body mass; guidelines: Stroud, J. et al. (2021) Methods in Ecology.

entries.label: Invasive Species Management Using Behavioural Ecology
entries.hint: Invasive species disrupt native food webs in part because native prey lack evolved antipredator responses to novel predators — naive prey hypothesis. Conditioned taste aversion (pairing invasive predator scent with nausea-inducing bait) trains native species to avoid them. Understanding the foraging behaviour of invasive predators (feral cats, rats, stoats) guides trapping placement and landscape-scale eradication strategies on island conservation sites.
entries.note: Naive prey hypothesis: Cox, J. G. & Lima, S. L. (2006). 'Naivety and an aquatic–terrestrial dichotomy in the effects of introduced predators.' Trends in Ecology & Evolution, 21, 674–680. Conditioned aversion in marsupials: McLean, I. G. et al. (1996) Animal Behaviour. New Zealand island eradications: Campbell, K. & Donlan, C. J. (2005). Conservation Biology, 19, 1362–1374 — 284 invasive rodent eradications reviewed. Kiore eradication from NZ using aerial brodifacoum based on home-range research. Invasive species now constitute 25% of all recorded extinctions (IUCN Red List).
